speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Freda, they're ready--

speaker: interviewer
text: And speed. Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Billy Frolick. Today is Wednesday, September 13, 1995. I'll be interviewing Freda Teitelbaum in English, in Los Angeles, California in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Go ahead, Billy. Take your seat, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freda Teitelbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-E-I-T-E-L-B-A-U-M. That's my last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm Spira from home. S-P-R-I-- [SIGHS] S-P-I-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never-- I never had to spell it, my [LAUGHS] maiden name. But, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you. And what was your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, August 8th.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your current age?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me a little bit about your life as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must say, as a child, I had the most wonderful time of my life. And my parents were very loving. My brother loved me too, surprising. But [LAUGHS] otherwise, really, I had a very good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Urich Spira. In papers, it's Julius Spira. And he was two and a half or three years older than I am. And what else would you like to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. We had very good friends, wonderful family, and we lived in a very nice section. So I was spoiled. My father did spoil me a lot. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents like? [BACKGROUND NOISE] Tell me their names, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Polish, it was Paula [NON-ENGLISH] Spira. And my father's name was Henrich Spira. They were very nice people, very intelligent people, and very, very loving family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an electronic engineer. And we had a-- I mean, by us, everything was we-- not my parents, OK? And we had a very beautiful business. The name was Firma Fala. It was Phillips Radio. Probably everybody would know what it is. And we have lots of other equipment, small music equipment, and gramophones with a whole wall full of records. And what else shall I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved in that as a hobby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I was. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always from school, I would stop in and play with the little gadgets. And during the war, when it war started, I was making Christmas lights on-- with batteries and small, little small lights. I even painted them in colors. So this was for the first year of war. And my hobby-- I like ice skating, [BACKGROUND NOISE] skiing, swimming, and I like to sit under the table and listen to the adults [LAUGHS] to talk. And this why I know so much about my family because when the war started, I was only 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear the adults say?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they started to talk in Yiddish, then I knew that it was about the children, so I shouldn't hear. [LAUGHS] But yes, I learned a lot. I learned about my family, about friends, and at about the plans for my future, my brother's future, and so on. And it was fun. It was nice. [LAUGHS] They didn't know half of it, what I heard. OK? Now, my brother loved to-- he had lots of friends, and when they played downstairs on the-- in the garden, I used to very often, I used to spray water over them. I was a mischief [LAUGHS] and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have lots of good friends. As a matter of fact, one of them is here from Cracow. And we love each other very much. We don't see each other often, but we talk to each other very often. And there wouldn't be anything that I wouldn't do for or she for me. If she was sick, even during the night, I would get up and run to her and vice versa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-- Irene. And let's leave it at that because she doesn't want to be mentioned, and she doesn't want to be interviewed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I asked her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the rest of your life as a child. Were you involved in a Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. My grandfather was a very pious Jew. And he was a very charitable. People believed that when children were sick, they would call him and ask him to stay with the child. No child would die on him. And they really believed in that, and I was very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your grandfather do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he studied Torah. His wife, Sheba-- in Polish, Sabina-- was running a very big store. And normally, at that time, the woman was working [LAUGHS] and the-- and the husband was staying in the shul or synagogue, whatever you want to say. And they were very involved in Torah. And that was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your synagogue was called or anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have a [INAUDIBLE]. We had more-- it wasn't as orthodox as my grandfather's synagogue. But the women were separate and men were separate. And I was always staying with my father when there was a prayer because he was a kohen. I don't know if you know what it is. [LAUGHS] And he had to get up at certain time, I think it was Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur. And he blessed the whole-- with other kohens, the whole congregation. And I was very, very proud of it because the people around my father's seat would tell me, don't look. You can't look. But I said, this is my father. I can look. [LAUGHS] And then we had middle class type of society, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other Jewish rituals or celebrations you were involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Each and every one. We were very much in Jewish life, but not strictly Orthodox the way my mother's family was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother was Bar Mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had quite a big apartment. And I know that we had one cook there-- special cook ordered and two waitresses. And I loved to stand next to him and take little pieces off the table. [LAUGHS] My brother got-- was very, very good in the synagogue. And she got beautiful-- he got beautiful presents that I wanted to open. What else shall [LAUGHS] I tell you? You will hate me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of presents did he get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he got from his grandmother that passed away and left it for him a beautiful signet ring. And he got an encyclopedia. He got the cards, and my uncle promised to teach him how to play bridge. And things like that and lots of books. And I would like to have sip of water because my lips are getting dry. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go right ahead. Tell me about some of the other people at the bar mitzvah. You had uncles, aunts, people like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But this was in our house. Of course, they didn't let me go to the synagogue because I was too little. But the-- the dinner, which was actually during the-- between 1:00 and 3:00 where here, dinner is in the evening. But I mention dinner because the food was so much more like today's dinners. And we didn't have any entertainment. No music. My mother played on the piano a few pieces, you know, at his party and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember which pieces she played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Paderewski, and the other one was Bach. And my mother was a very good pianist, but for herself not for-- matter of fact, you have two pictures where she was sitting at the piano. I think you took the two pictures. And she was a very nice lady. She loved us. She always looked that everything is clean and tastes good. And that's it. Then we used to go-- every summer, we used to go for vacations. And twice, we went to it called [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a place on the Baltic. And we used to bring home shells and pieces of amber that were thrown out with the waves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what else? Then we used to go for winter time to Zakopane. We used the skis there, even with my parents. And I loved it. And that time, I remember my mother used to go to [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you know what it is-- a dance that was very modern at the time. It was probably '36 or something like that. Now what else do you want to know? Tell me about your aunts and uncles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one uncle who was very Orthodox. But for Easter, he would bring me sugar little eggs and sugar little, you know, lamb, or whatever goes with regular Easter, not Jewish Easter. And then one uncle that was-- that lived with us for a long time here. He would-- on the way from his business to our house, somehow we always met together and then he would take me for cakes or cookies or whatever, and my mother said, don't eat it before the meal. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And what else? I had one aunt and I called her my second mother because I liked to go there and sleep over. I was gypsy. [LAUGHS] And sometimes my father would come and pick me up and take me home and said, you don't sleep every day there. [LAUGHS] What else shall I tell you? I was going to the Institute of Music twice a week, and I learned how to play on the piano. I was pretty good for my age. And then we had beautiful [NON-ENGLISH], we called it. Those are like gardens that went through the Cracow, and they had beautiful two restaurants there. Actually, it was everything like outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was a place where we played-- where we played ball. And then there was the place where we made believe that we are the big people and have all kinds of talks and stuff like that, you know. But this is everything before I was 13, up to 13. And then it started, the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to regular school, six grades. After six grades, I went to a Jewish gymnasium. And I only had one year of schooling because then, the German walked in into Cracow, took over the Cracow. And Jewish children had no right to go to school anymore. My mother gave them our-- one of our rooms. And there was few students that would come and learn. And all the teachers from the school would come and teach us. And this was done so that nobody would see when we walked in or walked out, or something like that because this was not allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first learn about this, and how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: That the system changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right away when they got in. Cracow was not destroyed because Cracow had lots of beautiful churches and castle and lots of museums and stuff like that. So they didn't want to-- both, both sides didn't want to ruin it because there was some arts that they right away through took to Germany. They, I mean Germans, OK? And they really took apart one church-- and this was the biggest church in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to have to store closed for a few days, but then my mother opened it. But I was the one that knew where merchandise was from being always in the store. Every day after the school, I would say, hi, Dad. May I come in? And then my father volunteered. He was an officer in Polish army. And because of his surgeries, they released him. He didn't have to go to war, but he volunteered. And that was the last time I saw my father. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My was taken-- my brother left the house with his uncles toward Russia. Lots of people were running away just before-- or just the first day since the Germans took over and they didn't know what was going on. But I think he left about two days before they entered Cracow. And they went to Lvov, which some people know as Leipzig because this was in German. Lvov was in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Germans were getting closer, then my mother was very unhappy. She didn't want him to go with his uncles to deep Russia. She wanted him back in Cracow. And we had a maid that used to go twice, and she couldn't get through. And the third time, she brought my brother home. And the first thing he would say, I want eggs. Can you make ten for me? But-- How long was he gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was gone about few months, not much longer. Then after he got in, my uncle-- there were two uncles and-- three uncles and one cousin. And they continued to go deeper into the Russia. They didn't want to come back to Cracow or any place where Germans were. But one of the uncles did-- somehow, he did get through a river. There was a River Bug, and he did get to Cracow. And he was our survivor as, you know, he took over everything. And in our house, we had two aunts with children, and this uncle. And everybody had a space there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Germans came, you knew they were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out? I mean, who told you and where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard shooting. There was one or two days bombs, bombing. And I was very scared. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- first of all, I saw one Chevrolet-- I mean, how do you call this open car?

speaker: interviewer
text: Convertible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Convertible. And there were women in, in nursing outfits and one holded a man, and it looked like his lungs were out of his body, and she was holding like that, and they were rushing to hospitals. And this made me so scared. I couldn't get it out of my mind. And then, we used to go during the two days, into the cellar, and we had chairs there and everything, something like that. And some people were talking about gas, that the Germans will use gas. And I was so terrorized by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So before my father left Cracow with the-- he was in the magazines in-- with soldiers, and so on. And he came in and he says, don't worry. I give you my mask. And so then this kept me. [LAUGHS] And what else did he give me? Oh, he gave me also a little pouch with medication, and he says you guard it because maybe it will be needed. So that's what it was. So that was the last time you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think was happening? I know you were scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going through your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared. Well, the gas that will be dropped and the shooting and, and everything like that. But before my father left, he told my mother, look, those are intelligent people. They are not going to touch women and children. It didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We all believed in that until they started to kill and take people in, in-- first, it was a prison that was only for political people. And I didn't know about it, OK? For me, this was something new. But when my brother came back after maybe few weeks, the Gestapo came in our house-- first in the store because we had lots of equipment that people could listen out of the country even, with the radio equipment. But they looked for my father. And because the father couldn't be found because he was away, then they took my brother who was only 15 years old. This was the man. I remember he-- they went to our house. We were very close between the store, with the business and house. And they asked my mother to stay in the business, and I was that time there. And they say, don't go with us. And then when they left, the maid was called our store that we should come right away. They beat my brother very badly, and, and they left him with-- you know, I never thought about those things till now. And he was supposed to go with my mother the next day to Gestapo and bring-- he had a little gun it was-- yeah, it was a gun that he was playing like all the young people would play. And he beat him with that. And this was very painful because when his-- my mother came in and saw this swelling and all this-- well, it was very sad. And then my mother was so sorry that we didn't run away at that night before she went to Gestapo. And they took him to this prison first for a few, few weeks. I think two weeks he was there. And we were still in Cracow, not in ghetto, so it must have been-- and they took him in May or June-- this I don't remember-- and to this prison. And I have some letters that my mother wrote to another person there who was not Jewish, and she asked him if it's possible to send him something to eat and things like that. He says, you have to do it through me because they will not put anything for Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we learned what was going there. And there was one corner where my mother would go every day, and that time I was with her and my uncle. And my brother supposedly saw her and was screaming through the window of the prison. We didn't hear anything. On the other side of the building, there were cars standing, [SIGHS] taking prisoners to Auschwitz. And that time, he was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the beginning, he was together with his professors from the high school and some friends. I don't know why the young boys were there, but somehow probably-- Now at that time, there was no Jewish camp anymore-- I mean, completely not Jewish. Everything was together. But about beginning of November, middle of November, was somebody wrote to us incognito-- because they were afraid to be caught-- that my brother is now building the first Jewish place for Jewish people. This was not a house. This was not a barrack even. It was just under a-- just not even a hall. It was with-- I think that in the beginning, they had no roof or nothing-- just few sticks around the place. And was-- he got dysentery. And he couldn't do certain things what they wanted him to do. So he was-- he got beating.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got the telegram. I think you saw the telegram from Auschwitz. And this was the one of the first few days that they did send telegrams also to Jewish people. I don't know of anyone. But I left the telegram in Yad Vashem. And the persons that got the telegram, and I told him, I don't see that it said it was 1940. And he says, don't worry, we know it was in 1940 because later on, they had already barracks and Jewish people were not notified when they-- and they had-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, then after this telegram, my mother was notify-- notified by Gestapo that she can come and they can talk to her about how did he died. So they said that he was-- this was a heart attack. And then they told her if she will send-- I have in my mind 350 marks, then they would send us an urn with ashes. And they did. But this was the most tragic time in my life and it's hard for me to talk about. But I think I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the telegram say?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we should stop. OK.
